---
title: "GMS System Design: Microservices, Database, Kubernetes, and RabbitMQ"
subtitle: "Grading Management System"
author: "Yuvraj Rathod"
date: "2026-07-08"
description: "A deep dive into the system design of GMS, covering microservices, database design, Kubernetes orchestration, and asynchronous processing with RabbitMQ."
categories: [Software Engineering, Open Source]
format:
  html:
    code-tools: false
    link-external-newwindow: true
---

In the [first blog](../gms_1/index.qmd), I shared the story behind **GMS** and the problem it was meant to solve. This blog picks up from there.

Now the question was not just what **GMS** should do, but how it should be built in a way that was clean, easy to understand, and close to industry standard. I wanted the codebase to be easy for others to read too, because **GMS** was always meant to be open source.

That is why I started with system design, database design, and service boundaries before writing the full code.

## From the problem to the design

The story of **GMS** made the design choice pretty clear.

There were different jobs in the system:

- Login and user identity
- Course setup and role assignment
- Marks and assessment handling
- Grading policy and rules
- Analytics and summary values
- API routing and access control

Once I identified these different areas, it made sense to turn them into microservices instead of keeping everything in one large backend.

That was the first big design decision.

## Why microservices made sense

I did not choose microservices just because they sound modern. I chose them because the work itself was split into different parts.

For example, `auth` does one job. `courses` does another. `marks` and `policy` have their own logic. `analytics` is different again because it reads data and computes summaries.

If all of that lives in one place, the code becomes harder to manage over time.

By splitting the system into services, I could give each part a clear role. That made the project easier to build, easier to debug, and easier to open source.

## The services I identified

The main services in **GMS** became:

- **gateway**: The entry point for API requests
- **auth**: Login, user identity, and feedback data
- **courses**: Course creation, student enrollment, and course roles
- **marks**: Assessments, marks, and challenges
- **policy**: Grading rules, policies, and computed totals
- **analytics**: Course and assessment analytics
- **frontend**: The user interface

This split was not random. It matched the actual work that each part of the system had to do.

### Architecture diagram

```{mermaid}
graph TD
    User[Student / Instructor / TA] --> Frontend[Frontend]
    Frontend --> Gateway[Gateway]

    Gateway --> Auth[auth]
    Gateway --> Courses[courses]
    Gateway --> Marks[marks]
    Gateway --> Policy[policy]
    Gateway --> Analytics[analytics]

    Marks --> RabbitMQ([RabbitMQ])
    Policy --> RabbitMQ
    RabbitMQ --> Analytics

    Auth --> AuthDB[(auth DB)]
    Courses --> CoursesDB[(courses DB)]
    Marks --> MarksDB[(marks DB)]
    Policy --> PolicyDB[(policy DB)]
    Analytics --> AnalyticsDB[(analytics DB)]
```

## Database design

I wanted the database design to be simple, clear, and easy to follow.

Each service owns its own data shape. That is a useful pattern because it keeps the responsibility in one place.

### auth database

The `auth` service keeps the user identity data.

Main tables:

- users
- email_id_map

This service stores login and identity related information, not course data or marks.

### courses database

The `courses` service keeps course structure and role mapping.

Main tables:

- courses
- courses_role
- admin

This service handles who is in which course and what role they have.

### marks database

The `marks` service stores assessment and mark entry data.

Main tables:

- assessments
- marks
- challenges

This is where the core grading data lives. It also enforces rules like not allowing marks above the maximum allowed.

### policy database

The `policy` service stores grading rules and course policies.

Main tables:

- course_policy
- assessment_category
- grading_components
- grading_rule
- student_course_policy
- computed_totals

This part of the system is where the grading logic becomes flexible. It supports policies like best N, cumulative, equal weightage, and custom rules.

### analytics database

The `analytics` service stores summary and frequency data.

Main tables:

- course_analytics
- assessment_analytics
- course_mark_frequency
- assessment_mark_frequency

This service is built for fast reporting and simple view generation.

### Data flow diagram

```{mermaid}
graph LR
    Marks[Marks Service] -->|published marks| RabbitMQ([RabbitMQ])
    Policy[Policy Service] -->|computed totals request| RabbitMQ
    RabbitMQ --> Analytics[Analytics Service]
    Analytics -->|summary values| Frontend[Frontend]
    Policy -->|final grade values| Frontend
```

## Why Kubernetes was the right choice

Once the system was split into services, orchestration became a real problem.

That is where **Kubernetes** helped.

It gave me a clean way to run many services together, keep them separated, and deploy them in a repeatable way. Instead of managing every service manually, I could use manifests and scripts to bring the whole system up.

The repo already reflects this approach with per-service manifests and deployment scripts. That was important because I wanted the project to feel like a real production-style system, not just a local demo.

Some of the main benefits of **Kubernetes** here were:

- Each service could run independently
- Deployments were repeatable
- Scaling was easier later
- Updates could be made service by service
- The same structure worked well for open source contributors

## Why RabbitMQ was needed

Not every task should block the API request.

Some work, especially analytics and derived calculations, is better done in the background. That is why **RabbitMQ** became useful in **GMS**.

The `marks` and `policy` side can trigger work, and the `analytics` side can process that work later without slowing down the user request.

That helps in two ways:

- The API returns faster
- Background processing keeps happening safely in the back

It also makes the service communication cleaner, because the services do not need to be tightly linked all the time.

## Why this design felt industry standard

I wanted the system to follow a structure that other developers could understand quickly.

That meant:

- Clear service boundaries
- Separate data ownership
- A gateway in front of backend services
- **Kubernetes** for orchestration
- **RabbitMQ** for async processing
- Simple deployment scripts and manifests

Because **GMS** was open source, this mattered even more. If someone new opens the [GitHub repo](https://github.com/yuvraj-rathod-1202/GMS), they should be able to understand the system without guessing too much.

## The tradeoff

This design was not the easiest possible way to build the project.

Microservices, **Kubernetes**, and messaging all add more moving parts. But that extra structure was worth it because the system needed to be maintainable and grow over time.

I would rather spend more time on a clean setup than end up with a design that becomes hard to change later.

## Closing Thoughts

This design phase gave **GMS** its shape.

The database was split by responsibility. The services were split by work area. **Kubernetes** handled orchestration. **RabbitMQ** handled background work. And the whole setup was built in a way that was easy to explain, easy to deploy, and easy to contribute to.

In the next blog, I will move from design to implementation and show how I turned this structure into a real codebase.
